blocks.content: **Fetching** is the process of requesting data from another location on the internet, usually from an API. In the browser, **JavaScript** provides a function called `fetch` that allows a web page to send a request and receive a response, such as loading information needed for the page. Because this takes time, `fetch` returns a `Promise`, which represents a result that will arrive later, and using `async` and `await` lets your code wait for that result in a clear, step-by-step way without freezing the page.

When using `fetch` with `async` and `await`, the code is written inside an `async` function. The program sends a request using `fetch`, then uses `await` to pause the function until a response is received. After the response arrives, it can be converted into usable data (such as **JSON**) and then used in the program. This approach keeps the code easy to read because it runs in a clear, step-by-step order.

blocks.description: This section on fetching will explain how to fetch from an API. You can click here to read more about APIs.

blocks.content: Below you will find code that **fetches (requests)** a random quote from an API (dummyjson) and transforms the **data (response)** into a JavaScript object.

blocks.title: Try it yourself

blocks.content:
When working with data returned from an API, the properties you access in the JavaScript object must exactly match the keys defined in the JSON data. JSON stores information as key-value pairs, and when it is converted into a JavaScript object using methods like `response.json()`, those keys become the property names of the object.

Below is an example of how a JSON response is mapped in code to JavaScript object properties:

blocks.title: Quiz
blocks.description: Test your understanding of how async and await work in JavaScript.

questions.question: What does the async keyword do?
options: It makes a function run faster
It allows a function to use await and return a Promise
It sends a request to the internet
questions.explanation: The async keyword allows a function to use await and automatically makes the function return a Promise.

questions.question: What does await do in an async function?
options: Pauses the function until the Promise finishes
Sends the request to the server
questions.explanation: await pauses the function until the Promise is finished and the result is available.

questions.question: In the example below, what will be printed to the console?
options: The number of quotes in the database
questions.explanation: The code fetches a random quote and prints the text of the quote to the console.
